pattern: Ending an alternating pick at index i in an 'up' state only ever needs the best 'down' score among far-enough-back indices with a smaller value, and vice versa, so two value-indexed max structures that only admit indices once they are far enough back answer every position in one left-to-right sweep.
hints: For every index, think of two separate best scores: the best alternating subsequence ending here where the last step was an increase, and the best one where the last step was a decrease.

To extend to index i with an increase, you need the best 'ending in a decrease' score among indices at least k back whose value is smaller than nums[i] -- and symmetrically for extending with a decrease.

Query that 'best score among smaller/larger values so far' with two Fenwick trees keyed by value (one for max after an increase, one for max after a decrease), only inserting an index's scores once the sweep has moved at least k positions past it.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two far-apart indices form a strictly decreasing pair
statement example, four indices with distance exactly one
n equals 1 with the value at the maximum bound
k equals n: only a single-element subsequence is ever reachable, so the answer is the maximum value alone
every value is identical, so no two indices can strictly alternate and only a single element counts
values at the maximum bound alternating with the minimum, checking the sum stays exact at scale
a zig-zag array where k forces skipping over some of the natural alternation points

stress.description: random arrays up to the full length constraint with random k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, k]

stress.description: long zig-zag arrays with a small k, maximizing the number of valid alternation points
